coredata.dc:title: Ex-vivo effects of propolis quantum dots-nisin-nanoquercetin-mediated photodynamic therapy on Streptococcus mutans biofilms and white spot lesions
coredata.dc:creator: Hosseinpour-Nader, Armin
Karimi, Narges
Ghafari, Hassan-Ali
coredata.dc:description:
Background White spot lesions (WSLs) remain one of the most critical adverse sequelae of fixed orthodontic treatment, despite materials and techniques advances in orthodontics. WSLs seem to be a multi-factorial interaction including increased microbial plaque due to intrabuccal appliances that limit the oral-cleansing mechanism and change in the oral microbiome during fixed appliance wear. The aim of this study was to investigate the synergistic effect of propolis quantum dots (PQD), nisin (Nis), and quercetin nanoparticles (nQCT)-mediated photodynamic therapy (PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT) in the eradication of Streptococcus mutans biofilms and the remineralization of WSLs ex-vivo.
Materials and methods The cytotoxicity of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite on human gingival fibroblasts was evaluated using neutral red. Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation following PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT was measured. Enamel slabs were prepared and demineralized using a demineralization solution containing S. mutans. Demineralized enamel slabs were divided into 9 groups (n = 10) and treated in the following groups: 1) Artificial saliva (negative control), 2) 2% neutral sodium fluoride gel (NSF; positive control or treatment control, 3) PQD, 4) Nis, 5) nQCT, 6) Nis-nQCT, 7) PQD-Nis-nQCT 8) Blue laser irradiation (light), 9) PQD-Nis-nQCT with irradiation (PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT). Then, the surface changes, microhardness, and surface topography of the demineralized slabs were examined following each treatment using DIAGNOdent Pen reading, digital hardness tester, and SEM, respectively. After the determination of minimum biofilm eradication concentration (MBEC) of PQD, Nis, and nQCT by microtiter plate assay, the synergistic antimicrobial effects of PQD and Nis-nQCT were determined via evaluation of fractional biofilm eradication concentration (FBEC) index. The anti-biofilm effects of each treatment on S. mutans were assessed using a colorimetric assay. The virulence‑associated gtfB gene expression was assessed following PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT by quantitative real‑time PCR.
Results PQD-Nis-nQCT at 2048 µg/mL had no significant cell cytotoxicity on human gingival fibroblasts compared to the control group (P > 0.05). A significantly increased (7.6 fold) in intracellular ROS was observed following PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT (13.9 ± 1.41) when compared to the control (1.83 ± 0.13). Following each treatment, the microhardness of the demineralized enamel surface significantly increased except for the artificial saliva (negative) and blue laser irradiation groups. The highest change in microhardness improvement was detected in the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT group (P < 0.05). Also, DIAGNODent Pen reading revealed the highest significant improved change in the level of mineralization degree in the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT group. Nis and blue light irradiation groups, like the artificial saliva-treated demineralized enamel slabs (control group), did not lead to remineralization (P > 0.05). Also, the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT treatment results obtained from SEM revealed that remineralization of demineralized enamel slabs in that group has significantly improved compared to the others. Light-activated nQCT, PQD, Nis-nQCT, and PQD-Nis-nQCT composite significantly reduced pre-formed biofilms of S. mutans compared with unactivated forms of test materials. The relative expression level of the virulence gtfB gene was significantly decreased (7.53-fold) in the presence of PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT (P < 0.05).
Conclusion PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT can be used for the eradication of S. mutans biofilms and remineralization of WSLs. The found in vitro efficacy should be tested further through clinical studies.

head.ce:title: Ex-vivo effects of propolis quantum dots-nisin-nanoquercetin-mediated photodynamic therapy on Streptococcus mutans biofilms and white spot lesions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: White spot lesions (WSLs) remain one of the most critical adverse sequelae of fixed orthodontic treatment, despite materials and techniques advances in orthodontics. WSLs seem to be a multi-factorial interaction including increased microbial plaque due to intrabuccal appliances that limit the oral-cleansing mechanism and change in the oral microbiome during fixed appliance wear. The aim of this study was to investigate the synergistic effect of propolis quantum dots (PQD), nisin (Nis), and quercetin nanoparticles (nQCT)-mediated photodynamic therapy (PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT) in the eradication of Streptococcus mutans biofilms and the remineralization of WSLs ex-vivo.

The cytotoxicity of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite on human gingival fibroblasts was evaluated using neutral red. Intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation following PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT was measured. Enamel slabs were prepared and demineralized using a demineralization solution containing S. mutans. Demineralized enamel slabs were divided into 9 groups (n = 10) and treated in the following groups: 1) Artificial saliva (negative control), 2) 2% neutral sodium fluoride gel (NSF; positive control or treatment control, 3) PQD, 4) Nis, 5) nQCT, 6) Nis-nQCT, 7) PQD-Nis-nQCT 8) Blue laser irradiation (light), 9) PQD-Nis-nQCT with irradiation (PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT). Then, the surface changes, microhardness, and surface topography of the demineralized slabs were examined following each treatment using DIAGNOdent Pen reading, digital hardness tester, and SEM, respectively. After the determination of minimum biofilm eradication concentration (MBEC) of PQD, Nis, and nQCT by microtiter plate assay, the synergistic antimicrobial effects of PQD and Nis-nQCT were determined via evaluation of fractional biofilm eradication concentration (FBEC) index. The anti-biofilm effects of each treatment on S. mutans were assessed using a colorimetric assay. The virulence‑associated gtfB gene expression was assessed following PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT by quantitative real‑time PCR.

PQD-Nis-nQCT at 2048 µg/mL had no significant cell cytotoxicity on human gingival fibroblasts compared to the control group (P > 0.05). A significantly increased (7.6 fold) in intracellular ROS was observed following PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT (13.9 ± 1.41) when compared to the control (1.83 ± 0.13). Following each treatment, the microhardness of the demineralized enamel surface significantly increased except for the artificial saliva (negative) and blue laser irradiation groups. The highest change in microhardness improvement was detected in the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT group (P < 0.05). Also, DIAGNODent Pen reading revealed the highest significant improved change in the level of mineralization degree in the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT group. Nis and blue light irradiation groups, like the artificial saliva-treated demineralized enamel slabs (control group), did not lead to remineralization (P > 0.05). Also, the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT treatment results obtained from SEM revealed that remineralization of demineralized enamel slabs in that group has significantly improved compared to the others. Light-activated nQCT, PQD, Nis-nQCT, and PQD-Nis-nQCT composite significantly reduced pre-formed biofilms of S. mutans compared with unactivated forms of test materials. The relative expression level of the virulence gtfB gene was significantly decreased (7.53-fold) in the presence of PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT (P < 0.05).
PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT can be used for the eradication of S. mutans biofilms and remineralization of WSLs. The found in vitro efficacy should be tested further through clinical studies.

ce:section.ce:para: Primary caries or white spot lesions (WSLs) are the initial stages of demineralization or decalcification of tooth enamel, which results in the appearance of white gypsum stains on the tooth surface. The possibility of WSLs increases in patients with certain conditions, such as fixed orthodontic patients. The presence of orthodontic appliances and brackets changes the ecology of the oral cavity, and as a result, the accumulation of microbial plaque around the brackets increases due to reduced oral hygiene. Microbial plaques around the brackets release organic acids in the presence of sucrose and the other fermentable carbohydrates in the diet, which dissolves calcium and phosphate ions on the surface of the enamel, and further demineralization of the enamel occurs. This phenomenon leads to the WSLs around the buccal surface of the teeth within 4 weeks. It is stated that 30% of WSLs occur at the buccal level and its frequency is 40% in men and 30% in women. The prevalence of WSLs in patients with fixed orthodontic treatment has been reported between 2% and 96% [1–3].

It has been shown that in fixed orthodontic treated patients, the level of Streptococcus mutans, the rate of demineralization of tooth enamel, and tooth decay are higher than in the control group. GtfB is considered the main virulence factor of S. mutans that is responsible for the production of insoluble glucans from sucrose which forms microbial plaques and causes WSLs. The gtfB gene expression level, which should be reduced to prevent WSLs, depends directly on the presence of caries. It has been revealed that deletion of gtfB gene in S. mutans population resulted in a significant reduction of biofilm formation with minimal accumulation of the bacteria [4, 5]. The prevalence of lesions is inversely related to oral hygiene and has a direct relationship with fluoride use. It had found that the necessary measures should be taken for education and increasing the use of fluoride in patients with fixed orthodontic treatment will prevent the development of white lesions. Of course, the effectiveness of these cases depends on the patient, which is one of their limitations. This limitation is reduced in modern orthodontic treatments due to the use of antimicrobial adhesives, coated brackets, wires, and elastomers with antimicrobial agents, as well as adjuvant antimicrobial photodynamic therapy [6].

Antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (aPDT) is a treatment that uses special agents, photosensitizers (PSs), along with light to kill microbial cells in the presence of oxygen due to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS; e.g., •OH, O2•–, and 1O2). The PSs only work after they have been “turned on” or activated by light with a specific wavelength [7].

Among PSs, natural products have gained major interest among scientists nowadays. Quercetin (QCT) which can act as a PS is a flavonoid found in many foods and herbs with potential chemopreventive activity such as anti-inflammatory, broad-spectrum antimicrobial, and promotion of immunity effects [8,9]. QCT exhibits low bioavailability, while QCT in nanoformulation (nQCT) has efficient bioavailability by enhancing its dissolution rate, solubility, and permeability. nQCT is favorable for oral administration. QCT has two maximum absorption bands at 380 and 258 nm [10]. However, Pourhajibagher et al. [8] have shown that the nQCT has a maximum absorption band at 410 nm with a strong biological effect at micromolar concentrations after activation by light at 405 ± 10 nm.

Nisin (Nis), a peptide bacteriocin produced by Lactococcus lactis ssp. lactis, is a natural preservative for many food products. Nis inhibits many Gram-positive pathogenic foods-borne bacteria. Nis has been approved as GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe) at 12.5 mg per kilogram by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) since 1988 because it has long been used in food preservation (E234) without being involved in health problems. Nis can be used synergistically in combination with conventional therapeutic agents. Also, the potential for using Nis as an antimicrobial peptide to treat local infections such as skin infections has been reported [11].

Propolis (Pro) is a resinous mixture obtained from beeswax and is produced by mixing beeswax and bee saliva with exudate gathered from sap flows, tree buds, or other botanical sources. It has been found that Pro has a considerable amount of calcium and phosphorus which can be facilitated enamel remineralization [12].

Recently, diode laser irradiation has had a positive significant effect on enamel remineralization due to an increase in the Vickers microhardness and reduction in surface roughness of the demineralized dentin [13]. It has been found that laser irradiation increases the absorption and uptake of remineralization agents including fluoride ions, fluorapatite, and photo-absorbing agents containing calcium, phosphorus, and fluoride on the enamel surface. Thus it reduces enamel solubility and promotes enamel remineralization [14–17].

To the best of our knowledge, no study evaluated the effects of nQCT as a photo-absorbing agent containing propolis quantum dots (PQD) and nisin as an enamel remineralizing agent and the antimicrobial agent, respectively followed by diode laser irradiation on the reduction of S. mutans biofilms and remineralization of WSLs. Since reducing the risk of WLSs caused by microbial biofilms and remineralization of produced WSLs at the end of fixed orthodontic treatment is important in achieving a successful treatment, and considering the increased application of nanomaterials and aPDT in dentistry, the aim of this study is ex-vivo study of the effects of PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT in the eradication of pre-formed S. mutans biofilms and remineralization of WLSs.

The poly(d,l-lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) used in the double emulsion-diffusion-evaporation method for the preparation of PQD-Nis-nQCT were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Chemical (Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany).

In the current study a diode blue laser (ASHA Co., Tehran, Iran) with a continuous beam, at 450 ± 10 nm, 150 mW/cm2, for 5 min yielding 408.96 J/cm2 fluence was used for excitation of the PS. At room temperature (25 ± 2°C) the distance from the laser probe (7 mm diameter) to the enamel surface was adjusted to 5 ± 1 mm.
We received nQCT as a gift from Pourhajibagher et al., [8].

Nis-nQCT was prepared by the double emulsion-diffusion-evaporation method [18]. Briefly, an equal amount of Nis and nQCT (5 mg) was dissolved in 200 μL 1% PVA. 50 mg poly (L-lactic acid) was added to the obtained solution and the mixture was then sonicated for 15 min in an ultrasonic bath at 30 W to form a primary emulsion. The emulsion was added dropwise into a 2% PVA solution. The secondary emulsion was sonicated at 30 W, 40% duty cycle for 3 min. The produced emulsion was continuously mixed in a stirring plate (at 700 rpm for 3h in the fume hood at 20 ± 2°C) until all the solvents were evaporated. Nanoparticles were then resolved in distilled water filtrated using a 0.22 µm filter paper. The recovered nanoparticles were freeze-dried for later use.

PQD was synthesized according to the study by Rodrigues et al. [19] with a slight modification. Briefly, 0.5 g of propolis in 30 mL of deionized water was autoclaved for 6 h at 180°C. The suspension was centrifuged (at 15,000 rpm for 30 min), and filtered (0.22 μm). The PQD analysis was per-formed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI-Tecnai TF-20). The PQD was stored at 4°C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite as the photosensitizer

ce:section.ce:para: PQD-Nis-nQCT-loaded microparticles were formulated by the water-in-oil-in-water (w1/o/w2) double emulsion solvent evaporation technique as described previously [20]. The PQD-Nis-nQCT composite was dissolved in a solvent mixture of 70/30 ethanol/water.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM; ZEISS, German) was employed for the study of surface morphology and size distribution profiles of the PQD-Nis-nQCT composite. To determine the mean particle size, following the collection of the images using the FESEM, they were processed using the Image J software, Version 1.53, a powerful license-free measurement tool to determine the diameters of particles as described previously [21]. Also, Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to evaluation of the changes in the chemical composition of the PQD-Nis-nQCT composite in the range of 500-4000/cm. The absorption spectrum of the PQD-Nis-nQCT composite was carried out by a UV-visible spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytotoxicity of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite using the neutral red assay
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the PQD-Nis-nQCT composite was documented by a quantitative neutral red spectrophotometric test [22]. The seeded human gingival fibroblasts (HGFs; IBRC C10459) in wells of a 96-well plate at an initial density of 1 × 104 cells per well were treated with 100 μL of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite (at a final concentration of 2048 μg/mL). The microplate was incubated at optimal conditions. Then, the neutral red solution (0.33%) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h. Then, cells were rinsed with formaldehyde (0.5%)-CaCl2 (0.1%) as a fixative. After adding 150 μL of acetic acid-ethanol solution (1% acetic acid in 50% ethanol) to each well, the absorbance was recorded at 550 nm.

The intracellular ROS generation following PQD, Nis, nQCT, PQD-Nis-nQCT composite, blue laser, PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT treatment was determined using fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) as follows [8]:

Pre-formed S. mutans biofilms in wells of the 96-well microtiter plate were treated with 200 μL of PQD at sub-MBEC doses in dark for 5 min. The wells were washed twice with 200 μL of sterile PBS to remove the loose attached and planktonic bacterial cells. 10 μM DCFH-DA (200 μL) was added to each well for 1 h. The fluorescence intensities of DCF were then measured with excitation and emission wavelengths of 485 and 530 nm, respectively. The intracellular ROS induction following n-QCT, Nis, and PQD-Nis-nQCT composite treatments was assessed as described for PQD-treated pre-formed S. mutans biofilms.

ROS quantifications following blue light irradiation were done in 1 cm × 1 cm × 3 cm glass cuvettes with pre-formed biofilms. The S. mutans biofilms were washed twice with 2000 μL of sterile PBS (pH 7) to remove the loose attached and planktonic bacterial cells. After treatment of biofilms with 10 μM DCFH-DA for 1 h, the blue laser prob stood on the top of the cuvette. This permitted blue laser irradiation during the capture of a fluorescence signal in a real-time. The blue laser system was switched on immediately before the beginning of the capture of fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity of DCF was then measured as the same above.

ROS measurements following aPDT were done after treatment biofilms with sub-MBEC doses of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite similar to PQD group and were then irradiated similarly to the blue laser group as mentioned above.

ROS quantifications were per-formed in the positive and negative control groups as in the PQD group, except that sterile normal saline and 0.2% CHX were used instead of N-QCT in the negative and positive control groups, respectively.

In the current study to simulate the clinical development of WSLs, an experimental biofilm-induced WSLs model on enamel slabs was used under ex-vivo-controlled conditions. Enamel slabs were prepared from human incisors that had been expelled due to orthodontic treatments and intended to be discarded. Using extracted teeth was approved by the Tehran University of Medical Sciences (TUMS) Research Ethics Committee (Application No. IR.TUMS.DENTISTRY.REC.1401.006). Diamond universal polishing paste (Maquira, brazil) was applied with a nylon brush on the labial enamel surface and polished with Jiffy Goat Hair brush using a low-speed handpiece for 15 s. Enamel slabs (3 mm × 3 mm × 1 mm) were prepared using a low-speed dental handpiece with diamond discs. Only slabs without decay, fractures, and cracks were included. The slabs were sterilized using gamma-ray irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the experimental WSLs model on enamel
ce:section.ce:para: WSLs were placed in a cariogenic solution which was prepared as follows: BHI (14.8 g), yeast extract (2 g), glucose (4 g), and sucrose (8 g) were mixed with 400 mL of ddH2O2 and sterilized by an autoclaved at 121°C for 15 min. Each sample was placed in a tube with 5 mL of the cariogenic solution inoculated with of S. mutans (ATCC 35668) suspensions (50 µL of 108 CFU/mL). The test tubes were incubated at 35 ± 1°C for 9 days, and the medium was renewed every 48 h, without extra bacterial inoculation. An orthodontist evaluates the formation of WSLs according to a guide published by the international caries detection and assessment system (ICDAS) (https://www.iccms-web.com/content/iccmsusage/clinical-ociation).

To evaluate the effects of treatments in the duration of 3 months including PQD, Nis, nQCT, PQD-Nis- nQCT composite, blue laser, and PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT on eradication of pre-formed S. mutans biofilms on enamel slabs and remineralization of WLSs caused by microbial biofilms on enamel slabs subjected to:

Artificial saliva (as the negative control); The samples were immersed in sterile artificial saliva for 60 sec and then thoroughly rinsed with PBS (pH 7). Artificial saliva solution was prepared by dissolving Urea (1 g/L), CaCl2⋅H2O (0.795 g/L), NaCl (0.4 g/L), KCl (0.4 g/L), and Na2S⋅2H2O (0.005 g/L) in distilled water.

Neutral sodium fluoride gel; NSF (as the positive control or the treatment control); A 2% NSF gel (Avant Dental Supply, USA) was applied on the sample surface and remained for 5 min; then thoroughly rinsed with PBS (pH 7). nQCT; One layer of nQCT was applied by a sterile cotton roll for 60 sec after drying the enamel surface and was then washed thoroughly with PBS (pH 7) to remove any visible remnants of the nQCT and dried.
PQD; The PQD was applied similarly to group nQCT.
Nis; The Nis was applied similarly to group nQCT.
Nis-nQCT; The Nis-nQCT was applied similarly to group nQCT.
PQD-Nis-nQCT composite; The PQD-Nis-nQCT composite was applied similarly to group nQCT.

Blue laser (diode laser; DL); A diode laser beam (continuous, 450 nm, and 150 mW/cm2) was irradiated vertically on the enamel surface for 5 min. The distance from the laser probe to the enamel surface was adjusted to 5 ± 1 mm.

PQD-Nis-nQCT plus radiation (PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT); PQD-Nis-nQCT composite as a source of the photo-absorbing agent (PQD), a photosensitizer (nQCT), and an antimicrobial agent (Nis) was applied similar to group nQCT followed immediately by blue laser irradiation as in group 8.

It has been revealed that after eating a meal, the oral microbiome breaks down fermentable carbohydrates and produces acid, causing a decline in salivary pH. In this ex-vivio study, to mimic the oral condition, the "pH cycling" process was done as follows: besides the bacterial induction remineralization course, all samples stayed in the demineralizing solution (pH 4.5 ± 0.2) for three hours and in sterile artificial saliva (pH 7 ± 0.2) for the rest time (21 h every day). The three-hour-demineralization course equals the total pH reduction level after 3 meals [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the effects of treatments on remineralization of WLSs

ce:section.ce:para: In the current study prepared enamel slabs were mounted in self-curing acrylic resin (Paladur, Heraeus Kulzer, Germany). To avoid changes occurring during the process of hardening the acrylic resin, the samples were kept in cool bath water. The enamel surface of all slabs was polished as mentioned above (2.10. Preparation of the enamel slabs). The hardness of the enamel surface of the samples was evaluated using Vickers microhardness tester (FM-700, Future-Tech, Tokyo, Japan) at three points of the sample at 50 g for 15 s and the averages of results were shown in gigapascal (Gpa) [12].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of changes in porosity and roughness in the tooth surface
ce:section.ce:para: Laser fluorescence (LF) detection using a DIAGNOdent Pen was used to determine the level of porosity and the roughness of enamel surface in the samples [24] as described by Moriyama et al. [24]. Briefly, before scanning, each surface was blow-dried (5 sec) using compressed air. The DIAGNOdent Pen was calibrated based on the manufacturer's instructions. The LF scanning was done in the same manner in each treatment group.

The enamel surface of the sample's topography was examined by conventional scanning electron microscope (SEM; Philips XL30, Netherland) [12]. For sample observations under the SEM, the slabs were rinsed with PBS (pH 7.3) and the proteins of the specimens were fixed then with glutaraldehyde (2.5%; pH 6.8 – 7.4). In secondary fixation which was done in osmium tetroxide solution (1%) for two hours at 4°C, lipids were fixed. After dehydration of specimens through a graded ethanol series (30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 100% for 15 min each) for fixation of lipid membranes to prevent their extraction by solvents during dehydration and infiltration by Hexamethyldisilazane (HMDS) or liquid CO2, the dried specimens were mounted on aluminum stubs and coated with about 20 s gold-palladinum using a sputter coater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the anti-biofilm effects of treatments on pre-formed biofilms on enamel slabs 2.15.1. Determination of minimum biofilm eradication concentration (MBEC) by microtiter plate assay
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh bacterial suspension was prepared in Mueller-Hinton Broth (MHB) supplemented with 1% glucose and 1% sucrose and adjusted to 0.5 McFarland (108 CFU/mL). 200 μL of 20-fold (1/20) diluted bacterial suspension (5 × 106 CFU/mL) were inoculated into 96-well flat-bottomed sterile polystyrene microplate containing enamel slabs. Microplates were incubated in capnophilic (5% CO2) for 48 h at 37°C for mature biofilm formation on the surface of enamel slabs. After that, the slabs were taken out of the wells of the microplate. Planktonic cells and loosely bonded bacteria to the enamel surface were discharged by washing twice with PBS (pH 7.2). Then the effect of the agent against mature biofilm was determined as mentioned previously [25,26]. The rinsed slabs were placed into new 96-well plates containing two-fold dilutions (2048 to 1 µg/mL final concentration) of each of the agents including PQD, Nis, nQCT, Nis- nQCT composite, and PQD-Nis-nQCT composite in 200 µL of supplemented MHB medium per each well and incubated in capnophilic condition for 24 h at 37°C. After the antimicrobial challenge, the slabs were rinsed twice with sterile PBS for 2 min and placed into a new 96-well plate containing 200 µL of sterile PBS. The biofilm cells were dislodged from the slabs by sonication for 30 min with a sonicator (Branson 3510 sonicator, Branson, Canada). The viable biofilm cells in the PBS were enumerated using serial and drop dilution assay. According to CLSI guidelines, the MBEC also is defined as the minimum concentration of agents that reduces ≥ 3-log (i.e. eradication of 99.999% of microorganisms) in a biofilm growth compared with their respective state controls in similar conditions or the minimum concentration of the agents that yielded a viable microbial cell count at or lower than the limit of detection (LOD) of 125 CFU/mL. As recommended by CLSI guidelines [27] the fractional biofilm eradication concentration index (FBECI) was determined using the following equations: FBECI = (MBEC of agent X in combination)/ (MBEC of agent X alone) + (MBEC of agent Y in combination)/ (MBEC of agent Y alone). The estimated FBECI indicates whether the combination of two antimicrobials is synergistic (S) (FBECI ≤ 0.5), partially synergistic (PS) (0.5 < FBECI < 1.0), additive (A) (FBECI = 1.0), indifferent (I) (1.0 < FBECI ≤ 4) or antagonistic (ANT) (FBECI > 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the synergistic antimicrobial effect of the agents via evaluation of fractional biofilm eradication concentration (FBEC) index
ce:section.ce:para: To identify the synergistic antimicrobial effect of PQD+Nis+nQCT on established biofilms, the “checkerboard dilution method” was employed as described previously [26]. To determination of biofilm eradication by a combination of Nis with nQCT, the slabs containing biofilms were treated with the combination of PQD and Nis in 200 µl of two-fold dilutions in supplemented MHB. In total, 11 dilution steps of Nis (2048 to 1 µg/mL) with nQCT (2048 to 1 µg/mL) and 11 growth controls were assessed for synergistic biofilm eradication. The microtitre plates were incubated at 37°C in 5% CO2 for 24 h. After incubation, the bacterial cells were dislodged from the slabs into the PBS as described above. The 10 µL aliquots from each well were spotted on Mueller-Hinton Agar (MHA) plates and incubated at 37°C in 5% CO2 for 24 h. The FBEC is determined as the minimum concentration of agents in combination that completely inhibited microbial cell growth on MHA plates. To identify the synergistic biofilm eradication effect of triple combinations (PQD+Nis+nQCT) the slabs containing biofilms were treated with a combination of PQD (2048 to 1 µg/mL) with Nis-nQCT (2048 to 1 µg/mL) and growth controls as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT on pre-formed biofilms on enamel slabs using a colorimetric assay
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the results obtained from FBEC assays, the sub-FBEC (at concentrations of 1/2 × FBEC to 1/8 × FBEC) of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite were chosen to assess their potency in eradicating pre-formed biofilms of S. mutans in aPDT. Briefly, the enamel slabs harboring biofilms were washed with PBS to remove loss attached and planktonic bacteria, then they were treated with PQD-Nis-nQCT composite (at a concentration of 1/2 × FBEC to 1/8 × FBEC, 5 min under dark condition) and then immediately irradiated with a blue laser at 150 mW/cm2. Quantitation of reduction of pre-formed biofilm on enamel slabs was determined according to He et al. [28] with some modifications. Briefly, treated enamel slabs were rinsed three times with PBS and they have placed in wells of a flat-bottom 96-well microtiter plate, separately and 200 μL of 0.1% (w/v) crystal violet solution was added to each well for 20 min. Then the slabs were immersed three times in sterilized PBS and solubilized with 1000 μL of 95% (v/v) ethanol solution. Then the slabs were removed from the wells and the absorbance of the destaining solution was determined using a microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US) at 550 nm.

To confirm the effect of the light irradiation on the antimicrobial effects of the used components and composites including PQD, Nis, nQCT, Nis-nQCT, and PQD-Nis-nQCT, pretreated S. mutans biofilms with 1/2 MBEC of PQD, Nis, nQCT, or Nis-nQCT were treated with and without light irradiation (a blue laser at 150 mW/cm2) as described above, according to the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the expression of the virulence‑associated gtfB gene following PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT by quantitative real‑time PCR
ce:section.ce:para: The treated slabs containing biofilms were used for RNA extraction. The total RNA extraction was done using the GeneAll Hybrid-R RNA purification kit (Seoul, Korea) based on the manufacturer's recommendations. After the elimination of genomic DNA using RNase-free DNase I treatment, cDNA synthesis was per-formed by RevertAid First Strand cDNA Synthesis Kit, (both kits were purchased from Thermo Scientific GmbH, Germany). The expression levels of gtfB gene were analyzed using the primers with nucleotide sequences as follows: gtfB F, 5´-GCCTACAGCTCAGAGATGCTATTCT -3´; gtfB R, 5´- CCATACACCACTCATGAATTGA -3´ and 16S rRNA F, 5´- GCAGAAGGGGAGAGTGGAAT -3´; 16S rRNA R, 5´-GGCCTAACACCTAGCACTCA -3´. 16S rRNA as a housekeeping gene was used for the normalization of the qRT-PCR reactions. The qRT-PCR reactions were done as follows: initial denaturation at 95°C for 5 min, followed by 30 cycles of 94°C for 15 s, annealing at 60°C for 10 s, and 72°C for 10 s. Fold differences in RNA expression were calculated using the Relative Expression Software Tool (REST) 2009 software (version 2.0.13; Qiagen, Valencia, CA, USA) by the 2−∆∆Ct method, and the change equal to or greater than two-fold was considered significant [29].

The results of DIAGNODent Pen reading and enamel hardness were tested with SPSS 17.0 software. The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Tukey post hoc test were done to examine the normal distribution of obtained data and dependent variables, respectively. Tukey's honestly significant difference (HSD) test was applied for pairwise comparison of the groups. In the current study, a significance level of 0.05 was considered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confirmation of synthesized PQDs and PQD-Nis-nQCT composite
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, TEM images demonstrate that the PQDs were synthesized in the range of 4–6 nm. PQD-Nis-nQCT composite in microparticles demonstrated a mean particle size of 1.1 ± 0.3 µm (Fig. 1b). UV-Vis spectroscopy measures the intensity of light reflected from material and can compare it with the intensity of light reflected from reference material. The UV–Vis spectra of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite are presented in Fig. 1c. Accordingly, the absorption spectra of the synthesized PQD-Nis-nQCT composite showed a broad absorbance peak between 400 and 550 nm.

The FTIR spectra of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite (Fig. 1d, lower curve) were compared with PQD and Nis-nQCT (Fig. 1d, middle and upper curves). FTIR of Nis-nQCT (Fig. 1d, upper curve) shows a broad phenolic C-O-C band centered around 1020 cm−1, C-O-H stretching at 1360 cm−1 aromatic bending and stretching around 1630 cm−1 and 1460 cm −1. Similar peaks are fixed in PQD-Nis-nQCT composite (Fig. 1d, lower curve). FTIR spectra of PQD (Fig. 1d, middle curve) showed a peak characteristic -OH stretching at 3420 cm−1 aromatic bendings and the peaks at 2650 cm−1 are related to the C-H bond of saturated carbons. Similar peaks are shown in PQD-Nis-nQCT composite (Fig. 1d lower curve). The aforementioned characterizations confirmed that PQD-Nis-nQCT composite had been successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PQD-Nis-nQCT composite was not cytotoxic for HGFs
ce:section.ce:para: PQD-Nis-nQCT composite at 2048 µg/mL had no significant reduction in cell viability compared to the control group (P > 0.05). PQD-Nis-nQCT treated cells exhibited normal spindle-shaped morphological phenotypes as same as the control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PQD-Nis-nQCT mediated aPDT increased intracellular ROS levels
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular ROS assay revealed a significantly increased (7.6 fold) in PQD-Nis-nQCT mediated aPDT (13.9 ± 1.41) when compared to the other groups including PQD (2.71 ± 0.19), Nis (2.04 ± 0.14), nQCT (2.88 ± 0.21), PQD-Nis-nQCT composite (3.48 ± 0.23), blue laser (1.97± 0.16), and control (1.83 ± 0.13).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the effect of the treatment groups on microhardness
ce:section.ce:para: The details of the mean, standard deviation, minimum and maximum microhardness of treated enamel samples are given in Table 1 . In all treated groups, after demineralization, the microhardness of enamel decreased and after each treatment, the microhardness increased except for blue light irradiation and Nis groups in comparison with the artificial saliva as the control group (both P > 0.05). The results of the post hoc test for change in microhardness revealed that, a significant change in microhardness in the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT group in comparison with other groups (P < 0.05). However, microhardness was not significantly different among the PQD, nQCT, and PQD-Nis-nQCT composite groups (all P > 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the effect of the treatments on the DIAGNODent Pen readings
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table 2 , the highest improved change in the level of mineralization degree was found in the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT group at the end of the treatment duration. DIAGNODent Pen readings of the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT and NSF treatment groups were at the same level (P > 0.05), which were significantly decreased compared with the other groups including PQD, nQCT, and light irradiation (P < 0.05). However, no statistical difference was detected among the readings of the Nis and light irradiation groups in comparison to the control group (P > 0.05).

As shown in Table 3 , following intergroup comparison of microhardness and DIAGNODent Pen readings of the WSLs in intact, demineralized, and remineralized specimens, the results of the Tukey's HSD test showed that there is a significant difference between PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT group and other groups including PQD, Nis, nQCT, DL, Nis-nQCT composite, and PQD-Nis-nQCT composite groups (all P < 0.05). While no significant difference was observed between PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT and NSF groups (P = 0.651).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the effect of the studied groups on the surface topography
ce:section.ce:para: The topography of the treated enamel surface using SEM for each group was shown in Fig. 2 . In the negative control (artificial saliva treated) group same as the image of the positive control (severe lesions following demineralization process) group, the enamel surface looked ridges, rough, and relatively large and deep pit-like pores (Fig. 2a). As Fig. 2b shows, after 3 months of Nis treatment, the large holes and deep cracks have become slightly changed, and the pores and ups and downs with smaller diameters are still available. The SEM image of treated enamel with a blue laser revealed a partially smooth surface of the enamel. Deep pores and wide cracks were not obvious, however, depressions were still visible. Treatment with the nQCT (Fig. 2e) showed that there was no rough enamel surface and pores as well as the enamel surface was more leveled, but there are still ups and downs compared to artificial saliva (as negative control), blue laser, and Nis treated groups are smoother. The SEM image of NSF (as treatment control) and PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT looked similar: more areas of smooth enamel surface could be seen and deep clefts and large holes were not seen (Fig. 2j and h, respectively). After PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT treatment, deep holes disappeared mostly and the enamel surface was further leveled, and smooth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti‑biofilm activity of treatments against pre-formed S. mutans biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate whether the PQD-Nis-nQCT composite would be effective at eradicating pre-formed biofilms of S. mutans, we examined concentrations between 2048 to 1 µg/mL of PQD, Nis, and nQCT. PQD, Nis, and nQCT achieved > 99.99% (i.e. 3-log reduction) killing of bacterial cells in biofilms at concentrations of 1024, 512, and 2048 µg/mL, respectively.

FBEC index of double (Nis+nQCT) and triple (PQD+Nis+nQCT) combinations were 0.381 and 0.312, which indicates that both combinations are synergistic. In this way, when Nis was combined with nQCT (Nis-nQCT composite) and also Nis-nQCT was combined with PQD (PQD-Nis-nQCT composite), they exhibited a synergistic effect against S. mutans biofilms in the current study. According to the FBEC index (Table 4 ), the concentration of Nis, nQCT, and PQD in combination was decreased from 512 to 32 µg/mL (16-fold reduction), 2048 to 125 (8-fold reduction), and 1024 to 256 (4-fold reduction), respectively in comparison with its original MBEC values.

As shown in Table 5 , light-activated nQCT, PQD, Nis-nQCT, and PQD-Nis-nQCT composite at 1/2 MBEC significantly reduced pre-formed biofilms of S. mutans compared with unactivated forms of test materials. While there was no difference between the OD reduction in the S. mutans biofilms treated with Nis only (without light irradiation; 1.59), versus the OD reduction in the S. mutans biofilms that were treated with Nis plus light irradiation (1.39; P > 0.05). Thus, antimicrobial effects of all components including nQCT, PQD, Nis-nQCT, and PQD-Nis-nQCT except Nis, were significantly increased by the light irradiation (all P < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti‑biofilm activity of PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT against pre-formed S. mutans biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: aPDT using sub-FBECI doses of PQD-Nis-nQCT (i.e. at 8 and 4 µg/mL) plus irradiation of blue laser for 60 s achieved >99.999% and >99.99% (i.e. 4- and 3-log reduction, respectively) killing of S. mutans in biofilms. Although the pre-formed biofilms treated with sub-FBECI doses of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite (at 8 µg/mL) alone and blue laser also showed similar results with decreased cell viability, the rate of biofilm degradation was not considered in comparison with the untreated control group (P > 0.05).

QRT-PCR data revealed that the PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT group downregulated the gtfB gene. 1/8 × FBECI doses of PQD-Nis-nQCT (i.e. at 2 µg/mL) plus blue laser could decrease the gene expression levels of gtfB by 7.53-fold (P < 0.05).

It has been revealed in previous studies that during fixed orthodontic treatment, the bonded appliances make it difficult to maintain oral hygiene, which leads to the accumulation of microbial plaque, and the release of several by-products such as lactic acid from sucrose, demineralization, and formation of WSLs [1]. Therefore, a procedure that robustly reduces microbial biofilm formation, while enhancing the remineralization of WSLs, is highly desirable. The aim of the current study was to evaluate the anti-biofilm and remineralization effect of PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT on S. mutans-induced demineralized WSLs by comparing the mean of changes in biofilms, surface changes, enamel microhardness, and superficial topography in 12 weeks after initial demineralization. In the current study, we observed that PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT can be effective against pre-formed S. mutans biofilms and significant reduction its virulence due to downregulation of the gtfB gene.

Diode laser was used in the current study because it has unique properties including highly monochromatic light with a very narrow bandwidth (coherent), the capability of high power, and adaptability to various source fibers. While other light sources such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and lamps have disadvantages including thermal effects, low power, broad spectral width, and large beam divergence. It is reminded that lasers, unlike LEDs and lamps, have disadvantages, such as being expensive and requiring high maintenance [30].

Nisin has been well-studied for its antimicrobial potency as a natural preservative for many food products and in combination with other antimicrobials [11]. Nisin inhibits many Gram-positive microorganisms including S. mutans. Pourhajibagher et al. [31], in their study on wound healing, evaluated the effect of Cur-NisNp-mediated aPDT including curcumin nanoparticles and nisin and both these materials had an antimicrobial effect. QCT as a natural photosensitizer with a flavonoid structure of plant origin, has a wide range of pharmacological activities, including antimicrobial effects [9]. The obtained results on the anti-biofilm activity of QCT agreed with Pourhajibagher et al. [8] who found significant degradation of pre-formed S. mutans biofilms following QCT treatment.

Our obtained data agreed with the results of multiple studies that proved the antibacterial activity of propolis and its extracts against Gram-negative and Gram-positive strains. In this study, the FBEC index of double (Nis+nQCT) and triple (PQD+Nis+nQCT) combinations were 0.381 and 0.312, which indicates that both combinations are a synergistic effect against S. mutans biofilms. The results of this study agreed with Pourhajibagher et al. [32] who revealed that the gtfB gene expression profiling decreased in S. mutans following ICG-aPDT.

Antimicrobial activities of the combination of propolis nanoparticles with photosensitizers including chlorophyllin-phycocyanin mixture (PhotoActive+) and toluidine blue O (TBO) were assessed by Aafrasiabi et al. [25] who compared the anti-biofilm potential of individual and synergistic effects of PhotoActive+ and TBO as photosensitizers in combination with propolis nanoparticles in the aPDT process (aPDTplus) against S. mutans and they stated that the combination of PhotoActive+ or TBO with propolis nanoparticles with the least cytotoxicity effects and the highest anti-biofilm would improve aPDT outcomes, leading to synergistic anti-biofilm effects and impairing the S. mutans virulence.

Newly anti-cariogenic and remineralization mechanism of chemical agents including casein phosphopeptide-amorphous calcium phosphate (CPP-ACP) as a calcium and phosphate store includes the incorporation of nanocomplexes of minerals in the tooth surface, in addition to its high safety level [33]. Cochrane et al. [34], found that the synergistic effect of fluoride and CPP-ACP in reducing the WLSs is related to the formation of CPP-stabilized phosphopeptide amorphous calcium phosphate nanocomplexes which increased the concentration of bioavailable phosphate and calcium particles. It has been demonstrated that CPP reduces the adherence, formation of microbial plaque, and cariogenic effect of streptococcus species in the oral cavity [3].

Although the PQD application proposed in our study differs from that in the report of Zale et al., [12] which describes the remineralization ability of propolis nanoparticles as evidence for potential PQD application as a remineralizing agent. It has been revealed that propolis enhances the availability and deposition of mineral elements including calcium and phosphate on the tooth enamel surface, thereby facilitating remineralization [35–38].

Remineralization is considered the repair or re-hardening of enamel, and this term is used as synonymous with the re-deposition of lost minerals in tooth enamel [33]. The microhardness assay of enamel is generally proposed for the evaluation of enamel mineralization [39]. In this study, microhardness evaluation was per-formed in two stages; the first one was done after tooth demineralizing and the second one was taken following the application of different remineralizing protocols. The insignificant difference between the treatment groups after the tooth demineralizing stage of the microhardness assay confirms the comparable tooth enamel demineralization process in the study groups.

In the current study, a significant difference was presented in the mean values of remineralization results between the tested groups except for the Nis and blue laser irradiation groups in comparison with the control group. The mean value showed high microhardness and lower DIAGNODent Pen readings in remineralization results for PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT group (3.51 ± 0.20 and 6.42 ± 0.66, respectively) followed by the NSF group (3.28 ± 0.37 and 6.91 ± 0.53, respectively) when compared to artificial saliva (negative control) group (2.41 ± 0.35 and 9.47 ± 0.34, respectively). The results of the current study are consistent with a recent report [12] in which the application of propolis nanoparticles (nPro) and nano-curcumin-based photodynamic therapy (nPro-nCur-aPDT) significantly enhanced the remineralization of demineralized enamel in comparison with the control group (artificial saliva treated demineralized enamel). However statistically significant difference (p > 0.05) was not presented between the tested groups at the baseline, but a significant difference in remineralization value was observed between the tested groups (p < 0.05).

In this study, the blue laser at low power continuous-mode was not effective to increase the microhardness of demineralized enamel which is in line with the report by others [12, 14] who reported that low power diode laser irradiation (450 and 810 nm at 150 and 500 mW, respectively) caused no significant increase in microhardness of tooth enamel. The obtained data of the current study are not in consist with those of previous studies [40, 41, 39, 42–44] which found that high-power diode lasers including Er:YAG, Nd:YAG, or CO2 promoted a significant increase in resolidification and remineralization of enamel crystals due high absorption band in tooth enamel [14, 45]. While the blue laser used in the current study was low power and low absorption band in tooth enamel. So, a photo-absorbing agent (nQCT) was applied in this study before blue laser irradiation to increase laser absorption and photochemical reactions by the enamel surface. In the other studies, blue eatable dye [14] and indocyanine green (ICG) [46–48] were used as photo-absorbing agent in combination with the low-power diode laser. In this study, an nQCT was applied because it was very cheap, more accessible, and less color than ICG and has more anti-biofilm effect in comparison with ICG and blue eatable dye.

Here, we show evidence suggesting that blue laser irradiation can increase the binding of PQD and nQCT to the enamel surface and lead to significantly increase mineral content including phosphorus and calcium in blue laser-irradiated tooth enamel and increased dental structure uptake of these minerals. The combined treatment of laser irradiation and fluoride application was proved to enhance the acid resistance and microhardness of tooth structures more than either of the fluoride alone [13].

However, our obtained data revealed that the blue laser irradiation through the photo-absorbing nQCT plus PQD and Nis (PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT) was more effective than NSF alone (as a positive or treatment control group) for increasing the microhardness of WSLs. This means that nQCT as a photo-absorbing agent reveals a synergistic effect of PQD as a source of minerals on the remineralization of incipient caries. This obtained data agreed with De Sant'Anna et al. [46] who shows that the laser irradiation through the 2% NSF photo-absorbing cream was effective to improve the microhardness of WSLs. The SEM images of the treated enamel surface with PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT in the twelfth week of this study also revealed that remineralization of enamel had a significant improvement compared to other treatment and control groups, which is in agreement with the reports that point to concomitant use of Er:YAG laser illumination and fluoride showed significant uptake of fluoride in enamel lesions compared to either blue laser irradiation and fluoride treatment alone [49]. However, within the limitation of the current study, further clinical trials are recommended to evaluate the effect of PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated aPDT and to speed up the repair process of the WSLs for better esthetic.

The combination of PQD, Nis, and nQCT demonstrated synergistic aPDT activity on S. mutans biofilms. Based on the biofilm eradication ability of PQD-Nis-nQCT composite against the standard strain of S. mutans, this combination should be studied further through clinical trials to establish its efficacy in the prevention of WSLs. The results of the current study revealed that the use of PQD-Nis-nQCT-mediated PDT can be improving the surface changes and microhardness in the remineralization process of demineralized enamel significantly and reduce the appearance of WSLs.

Armin Hosseinpour-Nader: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Narges Karimi: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Methodology. Hassan-Ali Ghafari: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.
